example.title: Sample Calculation
steps: Window Width: 150 cm, Curtain Length: 250 cm

explanation:
**DIY home and construction calculations** rely on proven material yields (square meters per liter of paint, bricks per square meter of wall, etc.). Based on IRAM standards and industry best practices.

## How to Calculate

Curtain fabric = (window width × 2) + curtain length + 10cm hem. The 2x multiplier provides proper fullness so curtains hang nicely when closed.

This calculator applies the formula with your measurements, checking against typical ranges and alerting you if values seem unreasonable.

## Final Notes

This calculation is a practical reference. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: How much fabric do I need for curtains?
faq.a: Use this formula: Fabric = (Window width × 2) + Curtain length + 10cm hem. The 2× multiplier ensures your curtains have proper fullness and hang beautifully.

faq.q: What does the 2× fabric fullness mean?
faq.a: The 2× multiplier is standard industry practice. It means if your window is 100cm wide, you need 200cm of fabric width for fullness. This creates natural folds when curtains are closed.

faq.q: How do I measure my window for curtains?
faq.a: Measure the width of your window opening in centimeters. Then measure from the top of the curtain rod to where you want the curtains to end (usually the floor or sill). Record both measurements.
The 10cm hem is the standard allowance for folding and stitching the bottom of the curtains. This creates a finished edge that prevents fraying and looks professional.

faq.q: Can I use different fullness percentages?
faq.a: Yes. Standard is 2× for everyday curtains. For a fuller, more luxurious look, use 2.5× to 3×. For sheers, 3× is common. Adjust your window width multiplier based on your preferred style.
Results are practical estimates typically within ±5-10% accuracy. Actual needs may vary based on fabric type, curtain rod style, and whether you add extra length for pooling or swags.
